calendar.matters: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Status Hearing For Filing Of A Petition For Final Distribution Or A Petition For Approval Of Report On Status Of Administration
Petition To Determine Persons Entitled To Distribution Of Estate

PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION, Appointment Under IAEA
TENTATIVE RULING: Continued to 7/21/26
EXAMINER: SEAN BANG

PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
TENTATIVE RULING: Continued to 7/21/26
EXAMINER: SEAN BANG

ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
TENTATIVE RULING: [Part 2 of 2]
The case law Movant cites in the moving papers to argue Objector has no standing as an intestate heir in this matter, even if he was equitably adopted by Decedent's father, has been superseded by statute: In re Calhoun (1955) 44 Cal.2d 378 was superseded by statute soon after its publication, in order to adopt the published dissent to that case, as stated most recently in In re Estate of Dye (2001) 92 Cal. App. 4th 966, 972. In re Esposito's Est. (1943) 57 Cal. App. 2d 859, as stated in In re Goulart's Est. (1963) 222 Cal. App. 2d 808, 823-24, "The Esposito case, in the light of the 1955 version of Prob C [sec.] 257, would seem to have lost its legal basis.'"
The Objection to the Petition for Letters cites Estate of Ford and facially pleads facts that attempt to address the necessary elements of the alleged equitable adoption of Objector and his brother by Decedent's biological father. The Opposition does not facially plead facts that specially address Objector's (or his brother's) standing as an intestate heir of Decedent, however. In the Opposition to this Motion, Objector cites to case law and Family Code sections 7611 and 7612, Probate Code sections 48, 50, 6402, and 6450 et seq, to argue how they do have standing in this matter, as interested persons, heirs, and issue of Decedent's pre-deceased parents. The allegations in the Opposition responding to the Motion's argument about Objector's lack of standing are not pleaded on the face of the Opposition, but arguably could be in an amend Opposition.
An amended Objection to the Petition for Letters of Administration consistent with this ruling shall be filed within thirty days of entry of this Order.
Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above.
Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing.
To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

EXAMINER: ELIF SONMEZ

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings By Petitioner, Laura Mccabe As To Objector, Dean Holloway?S Objection To The Petition For Letters Of Special Administration; Memorandum Of Points And Authorites
TENTATIVE RULING: [Part 1 of 2]
This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Russell S. Roeca.
Before the court is Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings by Petitioner, Laura McCabe as to Objector, Dean Holloway's Objection to the Petition for Letters of Special Administration ("Motion").
Per the Notice of Motion, the Motion is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 438, on the grounds that "Objector lacks standing as a matter of law because even if he were equitably adopted (which Petitioner disputes), such adoption would not make him an heir at law."
The court notes, as a threshold issue, under Code of Civil Procedure sec. 439, "Before filing a motion for judgment on the pleadings pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 438, the moving party must meet and confer in person or by telephone or by video conference with the party who filed the pleading that is subject to the motion for judgment on the pleadings for the purpose of determining whether an agreement can be reached that would resolve the objections to be raised in the motion for judgment on the pleadings." There is no declaration filed with the Motion demonstrating the parties engaged in the required meet and confer efforts before Petitioner filed the Motion. While failure to meet and confer is, alone, not sufficient grounds to deny a motion for judgment ton the pleadings brought under Section 438, the court reminds Petitioner to comply with the statutory meet and confer requirements - and all relevant statutory requirements - going forward.
The Motion is GRANTED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND, as follows.
A motion for judgment on the pleadings has the same purpose and effect as a general demurrer. Like a demurrer, a motion for judgment on the pleadings addresses defects disclosed on the face of the complaint or answer, and admits, for purposes of the motion, the truth of all material facts that have been pleaded (see, e.g. Tukes v. Richard (2022) 81 Cal. App. 5th 1, 18).
Here, Petitioner is the surviving biological sister of Decedent. Her Petition for Letters of Administration, filed January 23, 2026, lists her as the sole intestate heir of the estate, as issue of Decedent's pre-deceased parents, with no surviving spouse or issue of Decedent. The Objection to the Petition alleges that Objector and his biological brother were equitably adopted by Decedent's father, thus giving them an interest in the estate as intestate heirs of Decedent that is not reflected in the pending Petition for Letters of Administration.
Neither party cites case law or statute directly on point with the issue presented in the Motion: Is an equitably adopted child entitled to inherit from the intestate estate of the adoptive parent's biological children?
As the California Supreme Court stated in Estate of Ford (2004) 32 Cal. 4th 160, 165, cited by Objector, "[Probate Code] Section 6454 delineates the circumstances in which a foster parent or stepparent is deemed a parent for the purpose of succession, requiring both a personal relationship beginning during the child's minority and enduring for the child's and parent's joint lifetimes, and a legal barrier but for which the foster parent or stepparent would have adopted the child. (See generally Estate of Joseph (1998) 17 Cal.4th 203, 208-212[].)"

EXAMINER: ELIF SONMEZ

MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
James Bohm and Sarah Uliberri for petitioner Laura K. McCabe (via Zoom).
Thomas Wrobel for objector Dean Holloway (via Zoom).

The matter was called for hearing on Laura K. McCabe's Petition for Letters of Administration, which had been tentatively pre-granted. Mr. Wrobel appeared and objected, and advised the court that a written objection had recently been filed.

The Court continued the matter to April 7, 2026, at 9:00 a.m. in Department 204.

Judge: Russell S. Roeca; Clerk: Kenneth B. Hunt; Not reported (D204)
